comments.body: Hey /u/jacobhess13, link and content does not match the title.
That's probably an issue on Commerzbank's end. I am just retrieving the link and title on their site. There might be another post on the subreddit with the correct title and link.
No worries at all, just wanted to let you know.

submission.title: A total of 15,333 businesses in Pinellas County, Tampa are set to be affected by Milton which account for an average monthly employment of 214,887. For the entire state of FL, around 1.96 million jobs are associated with businesses in zones likely to be flooded by the storm.

comments.body:
Hi all,

A reminder that comments do need to be on-topic and engage with the article past the headline. Please make sure to read the article before commenting.  Very short comments will automatically be removed by automod. Please avoid making comments that do not focus on the economic content or whose primary thesis rests on personal anecdotes. 

As always our comment rules can be found [here](https://reddit.com/r/Economics/comments/fx9crj/rules_roundtable_redux_rule_vi_and_offtopic/)

*I am a bot, and this action was performed automatically. Please [contact the moderators of this subreddit](/message/compose/?to=/r/Economics) if you have any questions or concerns.*

It's ok! Today the markets could close at another all time high. "US100 increased to a 12-week high of 20274.00 Index Points. Over the past 4 weeks, US 100 Tech Index gained 7.6%, and in the last 12 months, it increased 33.89%." /s
The next unemployment report release in November 1st. Data is being collected this week. So it may have some impact on the unemployment report right before the election.
I mean I know it’s a little morbid but there is money to be made in rebuilding.  It’s not like a hurricane comes and all companies affected just close up shop and never do business again either.
Don't forget that wages have outpaced inflation since the beginning of records being kept, so if you're not swimming in expendable cash, you're a fucking loser!

It is a source of economic activity but it comes with a cost. The flood insurance premiums in this region of the world have been rising the past few years as outdated predictions on the avg number and size of hurricanes per year are being replaced with newer predictions that predict more frequency and severity. Due to Florida laws limiting increases in annual premiums several major insurance firms have stated their intentions to stop insuring properties in Florida all together. leaving most Floridians dependent on state flood insurance which is a drag on the state budget and has the state treasurer begging the Florida legislature to allow the state insurance to charge higher premiums lest they be forced to stop writing new contracts due to fears of insolvency. If the state insurance stops writing contracts and the private insurers leave the state most people in Florida will be unable to obtain flood insurance on their properties and as a result they will be unable to use their uninsured properties as collateral limiting their borrowing power. All of this is to say that while short term the destruction of properties will lead to an increase in overall economic activity. In the long term the costs of these hurricanes will ultimately lead to a decrease in economic activity in this region of the world.

>For the entire state of FL, around 1.96 million jobs are associated with businesses in zones likely to be flooded by the storm.

Imagine being told to come into work and doing it, even when you're likely out a job as the business is guaranteed to be shut down for weeks or even destroyed.

Rule III:

--

Submissions must be from original sources with original headlines. Memes, self-promotion and low-quality blogs are not acceptable. Source spamming is not acceptable. [Further explanation.](https://www.reddit.com/r/Economics/comments/80gcd0/meta_rules_round_table_3_rule_iii/)

--



If you have any questions about this removal, please [contact the mods](/message/compose/?to=/r/economics&subject=Moderation).
